meta.title: MongoDB, NoSQL & Big Data
meta.description: MongoDB, Redis, DynamoDB, Elasticsearch, Hadoop, and NoSQL data modeling

items.text: Is the choice between embedding and referencing documents justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Embed data that's read together and has a 1:few relationship. Reference data that's shared, large, or has unbounded growth.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong data modeling is the #1 cause of NoSQL performance problems. Over-embedding creates huge documents. Over-referencing requires expensive joins.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check document sizes and access patterns. Embedded arrays that grow unbounded will eventually hit the 16MB BSON limit.
baseContent.exampleComment: Embedding all comments in the post document will grow unbounded. Reference comments in a separate collection with a post_id.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Model for your query patterns. Embed what you read together. Reference what grows unbounded.

items.text: Is the schema designed for the most common query patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: NoSQL schemas should be designed around read patterns, not normalized like SQL. Denormalization is expected and encouraged for read-heavy workloads.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A schema designed for SQL-like normalized access will perform terribly in NoSQL. Multiple collection lookups are expensive without JOINs.
baseContent.howToVerify: List the top 3-5 query patterns. Verify each can be satisfied with a single collection read. Check for patterns requiring multiple lookups.
baseContent.exampleComment: The user profile page requires 4 separate collection lookups. Denormalize the data into a single document for single-read access.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design schema for query patterns, not normalization. One query should satisfy one read pattern.

items.text: Are document sizes kept reasonable (not approaching size limits)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: MongoDB documents have a 16MB limit. DynamoDB items have a 400KB limit. Keep documents well under limits to avoid future issues.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Documents approaching size limits cause write failures that are hard to debug and fix without data migration.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for embedded arrays that grow with usage (comments, events, logs). These can push documents past size limits.
baseContent.exampleComment: This document embeds an unbounded event log. At 1000 events per day, it'll hit 16MB in months. Use a separate collection.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep documents well under size limits. Move unbounded arrays to separate collections.

items.text: Are partition/sort keys designed for even data distribution?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Partition keys should distribute data evenly across shards/partitions. Hot keys cause throttling in DynamoDB and hot spots in sharded MongoDB.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A hot partition key means one node handles most traffic while others are idle. This creates bottlenecks and throttling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Analyze the cardinality and distribution of partition key values. Check for keys like 'status' (few values) or date-based keys (recent dates are hot).
baseContent.exampleComment: Using date as the partition key concentrates all today's writes on one partition. Use a composite key with a hash prefix.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Choose high-cardinality partition keys. Avoid keys that concentrate traffic on a few values.

items.text: Are indexes created for query patterns (not missing or excessive)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Create indexes for fields used in find(), sort(), and aggregation. Don't create indexes you don't need — each slows writes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing indexes cause full collection scans. Excessive indexes slow write performance and waste storage.
baseContent.howToVerify: Use explain() to verify queries use indexes. Check for unused indexes with $indexStats. Verify compound index field order matches queries.
baseContent.exampleComment: This query filters by status and sorts by created_at but there's no compound index. Create: { status: 1, created_at: -1 }.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Create indexes to match query patterns. Use explain() to verify they're used. Remove unused indexes.

items.text: Are aggregation pipelines efficient (filtering early, using indexes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Put $match and $sort stages first in the pipeline so they can use indexes. Avoid $lookup (joins) in hot paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: $match at the end scans all documents first. $lookup without indexes is extremely slow. Pipeline stage order matters enormously.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check aggregation pipeline stage order. $match and $sort should come first. Verify $match uses indexed fields.
baseContent.exampleComment: This pipeline runs $project before $match, preventing index usage. Move $match to the first stage.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put $match first in aggregation pipelines. Verify pipelines use indexes with explain().

items.text: Is cursor-based pagination used instead of skip/offset?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use range-based pagination (where _id > lastId) instead of skip(). Skip must scan and discard documents.
baseContent.whyItMatters: skip(10000) scans 10,000 documents to discard them. At page 1000, it scans 1M documents. Cursor pagination is O(1).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for .skip() with large or variable offsets. Replace with cursor-based pagination using the last document's ID.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses skip(page * limit) for pagination. At page 100, it scans 10,000 documents. Use cursor-based pagination instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use cursor-based pagination for large datasets. Only use skip() for small, bounded collections.

items.text: Are projections used to return only needed fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use projections to return only the fields you need. This reduces network transfer and memory usage.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Returning entire documents when you only need 2 fields wastes bandwidth and memory. Especially impactful for large documents.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check find() calls for projection parameters. Verify large document queries only return needed fields.
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns entire user documents but only uses name and email. Add projection: { name: 1, email: 1 }.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always project to needed fields. Don't transfer entire documents for partial reads.

items.text: Do cached values have appropriate TTLs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every cached value should have an expiration time (TTL). Without TTL, stale data persists indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing TTLs cause stale data and memory exhaustion. Cache grows without bound until Redis runs out of memory.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check SET commands for EX/PX options. Verify TTLs match data freshness requirements.
baseContent.exampleComment: This caches user profiles without TTL. If a user updates their name, the old name persists forever. Add a 1-hour TTL.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set TTLs on cached data. Match TTL to data freshness requirements.

items.text: Is cache invalidation handled correctly on writes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When data is updated, the corresponding cache entry must be invalidated or updated. Choose write-through or write-behind strategies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stale cache after updates shows users old data. Missing invalidation is the second hardest problem in computer science.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that write/update operations also invalidate or update the cache. Verify the invalidation strategy is consistent.
baseContent.exampleComment: The update endpoint modifies the database but doesn't invalidate the cache. Users see stale data until TTL expires.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Invalidate cache on writes. Use consistent patterns (cache-aside, write-through) throughout the application.

items.text: Is the thundering herd problem mitigated for popular keys?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a popular cache key expires, many requests simultaneously hit the database. Use locking, staggered TTLs, or cache warming.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Thundering herd can overwhelm the database with identical queries, causing cascading failures during cache expiration.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for popular cache keys with identical TTLs. Verify there's protection (lock, singleflight, or staggered expiry).
baseContent.exampleComment: This homepage cache expires for all users simultaneously. Add TTL jitter (TTL + random 0-60s) to spread recomputation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add TTL jitter for popular keys. Use locks or singleflight to prevent duplicate recomputation.

items.text: Is cache serialization efficient (not JSON for large binary data)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Choose serialization format based on data type: JSON for small objects, MessagePack/Protobuf for large data, raw bytes for binary.
baseContent.whyItMatters: JSON serialization of large objects is slow and bloated. A 1MB JSON cache entry could be 200KB in MessagePack.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check cached data sizes and serialization format. Verify large cache entries aren't using JSON unnecessarily.
baseContent.exampleComment: This caches 500KB of data as JSON. Use MessagePack or Protobuf to reduce size and serialization time by 60%.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use efficient serialization for large cache entries. JSON is fine for small, human-readable data.

items.text: Is NoSQL injection prevented (no user input in query operators)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: User input should not be used directly in query operators ($gt, $ne, $where). Sanitize input and validate types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: NoSQL injection can bypass authentication, extract data, or execute arbitrary code (with $where in MongoDB).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for user input passed directly into query objects. Verify req.body fields aren't used as query operators.
baseContent.exampleComment: This passes req.body.username directly into the query. An attacker can send { $ne: '' } to match any user.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate and sanitize all user input. Never pass raw user input into query operators.

items.text: Is database authentication enabled (not running without auth)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: MongoDB, Redis, and other NoSQL databases should have authentication enabled. Never expose unauthenticated databases to the network.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unauthenticated MongoDB/Redis instances are the most common cause of data breaches. Bots scan for open databases continuously.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check connection strings for authentication credentials. Verify the database requires authentication and isn't bound to 0.0.0.0.
baseContent.exampleComment: This MongoDB connection has no authentication. Enable authentication and bind to localhost or use a private network.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always enable authentication. Bind to private networks. Never expose databases to the public internet.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Configure automated backups with appropriate retention. Test restore procedures regularly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without backups, any data loss (hardware failure, accidental deletion, ransomware) is permanent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for backup configuration (mongodump schedule, Redis RDB/AOF, managed service backups). Verify restore has been tested.
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no backup configured for this Redis instance. If it crashes, all session data is lost. Enable AOF persistence.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Configure automated backups. Test restore procedures. Document recovery time objectives.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use connection pooling for all NoSQL databases. Configure pool sizes based on expected concurrency and database limits.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Creating connections per request is expensive. Without pooling, high traffic exhausts connections and the database rejects new ones.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check connection configuration for pool size settings. Verify the pool size matches deployment requirements.
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates a new MongoDB connection per request. Use a connection pool with maxPoolSize set appropriately.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use connection pooling. Size pools based on your database's connection limits.

items.text: Is the sharding strategy appropriate for data growth?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Choose shard keys that distribute data and queries evenly. Avoid monotonically increasing keys (timestamps, auto-increment IDs).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Poor shard keys create hot spots where one shard handles most traffic while others are idle.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check shard key cardinality and distribution. Verify queries include the shard key to avoid scatter-gather.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using timestamp as shard key concentrates all new writes on one shard. Use a hashed compound key instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Choose shard keys with high cardinality and even distribution. Include shard keys in query filters.

items.text: Are read replicas used appropriately for read-heavy workloads?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Direct read-heavy queries to replica nodes. Understand that replicas may have slight lag (eventual consistency).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sending all reads to the primary overloads it. Read replicas distribute load, but stale reads must be acceptable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check read preferences in connection configuration. Verify read-after-write scenarios use the primary, not replicas.
baseContent.exampleComment: This analytics query runs on the primary, competing with writes. Route analytics to a secondary read replica.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use read replicas for read-heavy workloads. Ensure read-after-write operations read from the primary.
